title: The Spark, vol. I no. 6, March 14th 1915

Vol. 1.

mnz FIRES OF THE NATION BURNING

(.5 - PARNELL)

Edited by ED. DALTOR.

No. 6.

DUBLIN, SUNDAY, MARCH 14th, 1915.

Price ONE HALFPENNY

IRELAND DOES REMEMBER!

Wednesday next is Ireland’s ¢ Day.” We shall
celebrate on  Wednesday, not the anniversary of
some great deed of empire, not some crowning feat
of arms or political achievement, not the anniver-
sary of the birth or of the death of some great king,
warrior, or statesman, or of some remarkable event
in the world of science, but the feast-day of Ireland’s
National Apostle—the feast of Padraig—the bringer
of Christ’s faith to Ireland.

On that day the hearts and minds of our country-
men and women will turn prayerfully to the face
of our great Father invoking His continued plessings
on this dear fair motherland we love, and asking
Him if the price of peace is to be denial and
betrayal, that He send again the Cross, and that
He give Ireland courage to bare her breast again to
the sword, rather than that her proud head should be
ever bowed in dishonour.

And her children in exile will turn their weary
eyes homewards to the mother, and the burning
longing to be near her in her hour of trial will eat
at their hearts, and shall be more than ever intense
because of the doubt and mystery in which her foes
have sought to enshroud her.

It has been told to them through hostile news-
agencies that Ireland has weakened in her olden
spirit, that she has succumbed to the tempters.
That she has grown weary of the path of righteous-
ness which she trod, and which had left her poor
and hungry, and that the bribe of material reward
had overcome her. That she surrendered her ideals

and accepted the kiss of her enemy. That she has
spat upon the graves of the hero-lovers who died
for her in the years that are past, and that she now is
content to don the second-hand trappings of Imperial-
ism and to go on a Maffick with Britannia as her
chaperone.

And they will wonder, they will only wender, for
they cannot be/ieye. Wonder that the historic Irish
nation which has so much to remember, could so
easily forget. Wonder if the Irish brain has
developed senility, and that when remembering
Belgium, Rheims and Scarboro’, it cannot recall
Bachelor’s Walk or Mitchelstown, or Manchester.
Has forgotten ’67 and the million famine victims
of ’48. No longer remembers Thomas Street and
1803, and rejects the memory of the peasant heroes
who reddened with the blood of Britain’s hirelings -
the fields and the hillsides of Wexford in glorious
’98. Wonder if crime, like wine, improves with
age, and if the treaty breakings and the massacres
in Ireland must, because of their antiquity, be re-
garded as nebulous traditions, or cloaked in the
mantle of imperial virtue.

Away with the thought.
land might forgive.

It could oz be.
Forget she dare not,

Ire-

Because in forgetting she surrenders memories
which are her sacred heritage—memories which
shine as beacon lights to direct her destiny, and
memories which God has permitted to her that she
might rear sons and daughters of heroic mould,
who could not be seduced from their faith through

2 THE SPARK

any bribe, but whose aim and ambition would be
to follow their exemplars, be the cost what it mlght
even to the death.

This is the thought which shall sustain our
exiles as they kneel in prayer on La Fheile Padraig.
They will discredit contemptuously the stories of
Ireland’s surrender, and Rooney’s lines will flash
through their minds as they turn their mental vision
homewards to the mother :

¢ All are not gone that love thee
Nor a/l fled.”

And we at home on this blessed festival will turn
to Padraig, the Ambassador of the Gael in Heaven,
and ask him by the memory of our martyrs to pray
for us that we may quit ourselves as men when
danger is upon us.

Ep. DaLToN.

2.
<Josle

The Song of Hate

The English press professes wonder at the inten-
sity of the hatred which Germany manifests towards
the British Empire.  This hatred does not take us
by surprise, we can quite understand and even
excuse it. It is only the very ignorant amongst
our own people who Aaze modern England.  Eng-
land is beneath the strong passion of hate, she
merits pity and contempt alone,
example of music-hall and pxcturc-papcr pamotlsm.
There is a complete lack of sincerity in modern
England. The English press is a falsefier of facts,
a propagator of brutal falsehood, and the instigator
of sham patriotism. It is useless wasting hate on
these people, we must strive earnestly to prevent
Ircland being reduced to a similar fate,

——tfe el

Crimeless Dublin

I am still anxious about the decrease of serious
crime in Dublin. I think that Dublin possesses
all the requisite conditions and accessories for serious

_ crime at present, and I think that there #s serious

crime and that the greatest criminals are the men
who are suppressing the news of it. Wil readers
of THE SParK send me particulars, for which they
can vouch, of women found dead in the canals or
women missing from home during the past six months.
Could my friends the officious “ G ™ men oblige?

She is the awful

THE DAY.

Air—¢ The Rising of the Moon.”
O, God rest you, Robert Emmet,
And God fest you, noble Tone,
God rest you, Hugh O’Donnell,
And O’Neill of brave Tyrone ;
God rest you, Patrick Sarsfield,
In your grave far, far away,
God rest you all who strove to speed
The dawning of the Day |

Chorus :
Freedom’s bright and blessed Day !
Ireland free from Foreign sway !
Lift your hearts and pray God speed us
To the dawning of the Day !

Not in vain you poured your life-blood,
Gallant hearts of ’98 !
Not in vain you stood undaunted
’Neath the scourge of English hate ;
Men of Wexford'! Men of Antrim !
Men whose names shall ne’er decay,
But shall shine like stars to lead us
To the dawning of the Day !
Chorus 3

O’er the fields your blood has hallowed,
O, ye hosts of Irish Dead !
In the light of Freedom’s morning
Men of Ireland soon shall tread.
When the foeman reels before them
In the thunder of the fray,
They shall shout your name in triumph
‘At the dawning of the Day !
Chorus :

Dpian na Danban,

The

Answer

A fortnight since I published the following
conundrum :—If it takes a2 European war to make
a Dublin Editor say his prayers, what would it take
to make him go to Confession ¢  Obviously, it
would take a much greater calamity, and according
to Princes Street the greatest calamity which could
befall ouf earth, since the Deluge, would be the
election of a Sinn Fein Lord Mayor for . Dublin.
I have a feeling that a hoary Jouma_nhstlc sinner will
live to witness that “ calamity.”

= A e £ S — S — . £ — —— S —? o — e ® e e — e g, — ! — A A

THE .  SPARK 3

THE RAGTIME PATRIOT

20 %%
afesfe——

Suburbia in Dublin has got it bad. On Sunday
evening I paid a social call on a family, the male
head of which is a “retired merchant”, in other
words he is an ex-publican, who having in the good
old days amassed a comfortable fortune in the slums
.of Dublin, thriving on the miscry and degradation
of the people, is now in a position to live respectably
in a nice old house miles and miles away from the
scene of his early labours.

He is not a bad sort as men go. He is incapable
of cutting a throat, or robbing a collection box, and
his great ambition }is to rear up his fine boys and
girls respectably, and, above all, to keep them clear
of the liquor trade. I have often asked him,* Why?
Isn’t it still a respectable business ?”” But whilst
unwilling to say anything condemnatory of the

business, he shows a quiet, stubborn determination

to notallow any of his sons enter it.

There are many such men on the outskirts of the
City, and the eagerness they display to keep clear
of old associations is quite remarkable. In their
young days they were the head and front of all
popular movements in their districts, and they
literally saw green, so intensely patriotic were they.
But now all is changed. Wise men that they were
~—and I use the word wise in that restricted worldly
sense in which it is used when only cunning is
implied—wise men that they were, they kept their
children free from that polluted atmosphere in which
the slum publican reigned as a social and political
model, whose power was limitless and whose word
was the law. They were wise enough to perceive
the demoralizing effect ' of that atmosphere on
character, as they had too well realized it in their
own case, and they had a sufficient remnant of
human nature left to save their children from con-
tact with the' mean, sordid, hypocritical life which
they themselves had lived.

Of course their patriotism in those aays was
shallow pretence.  How could patriotism, that
purest and noblest of passions, thrive in the breasts
-of men whose daily bread depended on the degra-
dation of their neighbours ? 'How could men'desire
the moral uplifting of their countrymen and women
when their own livelihood depended on their de-
moralization { It was a cruel demand to make on
a semi-illiterate body of men sach as the publicans
of Dublin were, and only men of extraordinary
moral strength could possibly combine the character
of publican and patriot ; ‘and I can assure you that

" Dolly Dyes”.

if there ever were any such, it’s not rearing respect=
able seonins in Drumcondra or Rathgar they ‘are
now, but most probably they are inmates of either
of your unions.

My ex-publican friead, then, is no great novelty,
there are many of him, and having become respect-
able he has welcomed a change in the political
atmosphere which also allows him to become
“loyal ”.. He no longer szes green.

When I was admitted to his drawingroom on
Sunday evening I thought he had converted that
apartment into an advertisement demonstration for
The plush chairs were covered in
red, white, and blue, the cushions in father’s arm-
chair were equally and as loudly luyal and his
daughtcr might have been posing for a sick
Britannia, as she reclined on a large Union Jack
which concealed a rather decently-upholstered sofa.
Even the doyly on which the flower vase rested
was a miniature flag of the Empire. I will not say
that I was struck speechless, but I certainly got the
head-staggers at the gaudy display. Why will not
people responsible for the ‘British Empire select a
decent flag, and not this abominable, cheap, and
vulgar combihation which revolts the soul of every
@sthetic man and woman. So great is my dislike
and contempt for these blended colours of red,
white, and blue that I wouldn’t ask a respectable
charwoman to make floor-cloths of them. ‘Leaving
political considerations altogether aside, and view-
ing the matter solely ‘as a' proposition in art, who
could tolerate much less respect and love a flag com-
prising such ‘an ugly and repellant mixture as this
horrible patchwork which Suburbia now, in de-
fiance even of its artistic sense, will suffer to trans-
form its drawingroom into a vulgar fancy fair ?

And how our own flag stands out’in ‘such clear
and vivid contrast—the holy blue of Ireland, with
either the harp or the golden sunset. I pat it to'my
ex publican on Sunday night : ¢ Are 'you satisfied
to swap flags with the British Empire? Do you
think the Union Jack}is a nicer flag than the flag
of Ireland, or do’you think it symbolises anything
more ‘worthy of more admirable?”” "But his only
answer was : Now, for''Heaven’s sake drop poli
tics, I know you're a Sinn Femer ; do you take
any water in it ?”

So when you meet a Sinn Fcmer, or when one
goes to Visit you, 'don’t argue ‘with him, because
he’ll get the'better of you. I #now because' I've
met some of them. The best thing to do is to
side-track him, or failing this hit him with a brick,
or tell a policeman he’s a' German ‘Spy or any other
hanged lie that comes to 'your mind. ' So' long as

»beu,get the Sinn Feiner shut up or locked up it’s
~ all right.

Joe Devlin says so, and Joe knows.

Isn’t it a pity that people who are otherwise toler-
able will allow themselves to be stampeded into this
Union Jackassery craze. The family I am dealing

~ with, with the possible exception of “poor stupid

old dad,” who is colour-blind, are really not the
blatant vulgarians which a visit to their drawingroom
would stamp them, but they vutrage all their pre-
conceived ideas of good taste, and succumb without
protest, to the Khaki epidemic on the suggestion of
the ¢ Daily Sketch ” or some equally preposterous
faking journal, which has undertaken to create a
¢ patriotism ”* which is not less spurious than the
ideals which we were to fight for in the war, but
‘which the ¢ London Times” honestly declared on

- Monday last to be all moonshine.

1 wish a colour censor were appointed, then one
might escape the risk of sore eyes, as it is I shall
have to have my glasses smoked in order tc preserve
my colour sense and senses.

E—
XT3

Qur National

Holiday

RELIGIOUS SERVICES.

Sermons and hymns in Irish will be the feature
of the services in many of our City churches on St.
Patrick’s Day. In the Pro-Cathedral Rev. Father
Gerald O. Nolan will preach in Irish at the 12
‘o’clock Mass, and in Clarendon Street An t- Athair
Tomas Mac Gearailt will be the preacher. I think
Irish sermons will be preached also at Mount Argus,
at the Catholic University Chapel, Stephen’s Green,
in the Capuchin Church, Church Street, and in St.
Andrew’s, Westland Row.

Some of the Protestant Churches also will have
portions of the service in Irish.

THE CONCERT.

I hope that Nationalist Dublin will excel itself
in its record attendance at the Rotunda Rink on
St. Patrick’s Night. If only for this one night in
the year let us give the foreign theatre the back of
our hand, and let the St. Patrick’s Night Concert
‘be more than a mere entertainment, Let it be a
great hosting of the Nationalist men and women of
‘Dublin gathered together under the one roof to hear
the song and the music of their land, showing by
their presence that dear old Dublin is where she

“always was since the days of Emmet, faithful and
unflinching, by the side of Ireland.

THE SPARK

THE SHAMROCK.

I remember some years ago hearing a Nationalist
say that he wouldn’t wear the shamrock again, since
Queen Victoria bad permitted its use in the British
army. I for my part viewed the “ honour ” which
that venerable sovereign conferred on the National *
emblem with amused contempt., These thick-
skulled English whilst caught red-handed in acts of
desecration and vandalism, will still innocently
wonder why they are disliked.  If we cannot pre~
sently enforce respect by foreigners for our emblem,
let us proudly honour it ourselves by wearing it for
what it is, not for what the English queen sought
to pervert it into.

s o%
B

Language Week

The annual collection for the Irish Language
National Fund will commence to-day, March 14th
and will be continued throughout the week. With-
out our National language we lack the hall-mark of
nationhood. It is our sure safeguard against our
absorption in time by a numerically stronger, even
though morally inferior people. It. may not be
given to us all to become proficient speakers of Trish,.
but even the humblest of us, and the poorest, can
do some service for the language in which Patrick
preached the faith to our fathers, during the coming
week. Help the collectors then by every means in-
your power ; remember theirs is voluntary work,.
and their reward but the consciousness of having
done their duty by the nation.

St. Patrick’s Night

GREAT NATIONAL
CONCERT
